items.q: Which auxiliary does 'gehen' (to go) take in the perfect tense?
items.why: Students default to haben (English 'have'). Verbs of motion or change of state take SEIN (gehen, kommen, laufen, werden). Most other verbs take haben. 'Ich habe gegangen' is incorrect.

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: How do you say 'I get up early' using 'aufstehen'?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. In present and simple past, the prefix SEPARATES and goes to the end: aufstehen becomes 'stehe...auf'. The prefix only rejoins in subordinate clauses, infinitives, and past participles.

items.q: In what year is the novel set initially?
items.why: It's 1958. “1945” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Coming to terms with the past
items.why: It's Coming to terms with the past. “Building a future” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Der Vorleser published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1995. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
